title: Asplenium × barrancense
intro: Asplenium × barrancense is a hybrid plant species within the Asplenium genus, commonly referred to as a fern. It has been traditionally used in folk medicine, boasting a range of health benefits including anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties, which can help alleviate conditions such as arthritis, wounds, and oxidative stress. The bioactive constituents of Asplenium × barrancense include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and other polyphenolic compounds, which contribute to its medicinal properties. Traditionally, this plant is used to prepare herbal teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are used to treat various ailments and promote overall well-being.
taxonomy: Asplenium × barrancense is a nothogenus, which is a hybrid genus of the Asplenium ferns, classified under the family Aspleniaceae. It is a hybrid between the Asplenium australiense and Asplenium trichomanes. As a nothogenus, its classification is not strictly botanical, but rather a convenience for naming purposes. The Asplenium × barrancense classification may also be referred to as a hybrid of the species A. australiense and A. trichomanes. The Asplenium × barrancense classification can be found in the context of horticulture and taxonomy.
